request.question: Who wrote the play "...but the clouds..." starring Billie Whitelaw and Ronald Pickup?

title: Leo the Last
Make Mine Mink
Charlie Bubbles
Rockaby
Rough for Radio II
... but the clouds ...
Hotel Paradiso (film)
Footfalls
Ghost Trio (play)
Ronald Pickup
sentences: Leo the Last is a 1970 British drama film directed by John Boorman, based on the play "The Prince" by George Tabori, starring Marcello Mastroianni and Billie Whitelaw.
Make Mine Mink is a 1960 British comedy farce film directed by Robert Asher and featuring Terry-Thomas, Athene Seyler, Hattie Jacques and Billie Whitelaw.
The screenplay concerns a group of eccentric misfits who go on a spree, stealing mink coats for charity in a Robin Hood-style gang.
It was based on the play "Breath of Spring" by Peter Coke, and its sequels.
Seyer and Elspeth Duxbury reprised their stage roles from the London production of "Breath of Spring".
Charlie Bubbles is a 1967 British comedy-drama film starring Billie Whitelaw and Albert Finney, and also featuring a young Liza Minnelli.
It was listed to compete at the 1968 Cannes Film Festival, but the festival was cancelled due to the events of May 1968 in France.
Rockaby is a short one-woman play by Samuel Beckett.

It was written in English in 1980, at the request of Daniel Labeille, who produced it on behalf of "Programs in the Arts", State University of New York, for a festival and symposium in commemoration of Beckett's 75th birthday.
The play premiered on April 8, 1981 at the State University of New York at Buffalo, starring Billie Whitelaw and directed by Alan Schneider.
A documentary film, "Rockaby", by D. A. Pennebaker and Chris Hegedus records the rehearsal process and the first performance.
This production went on to be performed at the Annex at La MaMa Experimental Theatre Club, and, in December 1982, at the Cottesloe, Royal National Theatre, London.
Rough for Radio II is a radio play by Samuel Beckett.
It was written in French in 1961 as Pochade radiophonique and published in "Minuit" 16, November 1975.
Beckett translated the work into English shortly before its broadcast on BBC Radio 3 on 13 April 1976.
Martin Esslin directed Harold Pinter (Animator), Billie Whitelaw (Stenographer) and Patrick Magee (Fox).
The English-language version was first published in "Ends and Odds" (Grove, 1976, Faber, 1977) as Radio II.
... but the clouds ... is a television play by Samuel Beckett.
Beckett wrote it between October–November 1976 “to replace a film of "Play" which the BBC had sent [him] for approval (and which he had rejected)” due to “the poor quality of the film”.
Donald McWhinnie directed Billie Whitelaw and Ronald Pickup.
It was first broadcast on 17 April 1977 as part of a programme of three Beckett plays entitled ‘Shades’ on BBC2.
It was first published in "Ends and Odds" (Faber) 1977.
An early title for the piece was Poetry only love.
Hotel Paradiso is a Metrocolor 1966 film released by Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer in Panavision.
It was directed by Peter Glenville and based on the play "L'Hôtel du libre échange" by Maurice Desvallières and Georges Feydeau.
The film allowed Alec Guinness to reprise the role he had played in the London West End theatre production of Hotel Paradiso, which opened at the Winter Garden Theatre, Drury Lane, London.
on 2 May 1956.
Guinness played alongside Martita Hunt (Angelique), Irene Worth (Marcelle), Frank Pettingell (Cot), Kenneth Williams (Maxime) and Billie Whitelaw (Victoire).
Douglas Byng also reprised his part from the stage play.
Footfalls is a play by Samuel Beckett.
It was written in English, between 2 March and December 1975 and was first performed at the Royal Court Theatre as part of the Samuel Beckett Festival, on May 20, 1976 directed by Beckett himself.
Billie Whitelaw, for whom the piece had been written, played May whilst Rose Hill voiced the mother.
Ghost Trio is a television play, written in English by Samuel Beckett.
It was written in 1975, taped in October 1976 and the first broadcast was on BBC2 on 17 April 1977 as part of the "Lively Arts" programme Beckett himself entitled "Shades".
Donald McWhinnie directed (supervised by Beckett) with Ronald Pickup and Billie Whitelaw.
The play’s original title was to be Tryst.
"On Beckett’s notebook, the word was crossed out vigorously and the new title "Ghost Trio" written next to it.
On the title page of the BBC script the same handwritten title change can be found, indicating that it must have been corrected at the very last minute."
Ronald Alfred Pickup (born 7 June 1940) is an English actor who has been active in television and film since 1964.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Role and goal: Summarize the first-hop evidence so the next agent can find the missing second-hop information. Produce a single short, factual `summary` that: (1) compresses the grounded facts in the provided passages; (2) exposes the bridge entity, the specific missing fact, or the retrieval direction needed for the next hop; and (3) gives concrete evidence anchors (quoted or clearly paraphrased phrases) that a retriever can use.

Content rules:
- Keep the summary concise and fact-focused. Do not attempt to produce the final answer; describe only what the passages support and what is still needed.
- Preserve the same passage facts and phrasing as you would otherwise (you may quote or paraphrase). Do not change which facts are exposed; only condense them.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from the passages to state a bridge or to justify a next-hop direction. Do not invent facts, infer new temporal facts, or treat composition as unsupported when the necessary premises are present.
- If the passages lack a required premise, or contain conflicting identity links, explicitly state which premise or identity link is missing or ambiguous so the next agent can retrieve or disambiguate it.

Date-format rule (narrow formatting change):
- When the summary includes a date token that appears in the passages (any calendar date or parenthetical date taken from the text), render that date using the canonical form FullMonthName day, year (for example, "September 27, 2017") inside the evidence description. This normalization only changes the string form of the date token; it must not add, infer, or imply any additional temporal facts, nor be used as a reason to produce a final answer.
- Otherwise, leave all other wording and evidence presentation unchanged. Preserve quotations or clear paraphrases of the same passage text; only adjust the date token's string format.

Output contract:
- Return exactly one field `summary` containing the compressed first-hop evidence, the bridge/missing-fact signal, and any retrieval direction needed for the next hop. Do not output final answers, extra metadata, or new fields.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact retrieval query string (single line) in the `query` field and nothing else. The query should be a concise keyword-style search targeted to recover the missing bridge or supporting evidence needed by downstream agents.

General behavior:
- Use the visible facts in summary_1 as anchors (album titles, artist names, dates, relation terms) and compose a focused query that is likely to retrieve passages supporting the needed relation. Prefer concrete anchors and relation keywords over full sentences.
- Do not include explanatory text, user-facing prompts, or requests-for-clarification in the `query` field. Do not invent or assert missing facts (for example, do not supply an artist name that is not present in summary_1).
- Preserve the single-query output contract: emit exactly one string and no other fields or commentary.

Special-case for missing album artist or genre (narrow semantic boundary):
- If summary_1 shows an album title but does not include the album's artist or genre, or if summary_1 explicitly signals inability to determine genre or explicitly asks for the artist/passage (phrases such as "can't determine" or "please provide the artist"), then emit exactly one compact search phrase formatted as:
  <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
  Use only the visible album title as it appears in summary_1 followed by the three keywords: album artist genre. Do not add punctuation, explanatory words, alternate tokens, or candidate artist names.
- Do not apply this special-case if summary_1 already contains the artist or genre, or if the album anchor is an ambiguous short token that requires human clarification.

Fallback and downstream behavior:
- If retrieval using the emitted query returns no useful passages, the downstream agent may then ask the user for clarification or additional context. Do not ask the user inside the `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Task: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` and produce a single concise `summary` that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when the post-retrieval evidence is genuinely insufficient, ambiguous, or conflicting.

Guidelines:
- Treat a concise answer in `context` as the primary claim. Do not replace that concise context answer unless the `passages` contain a direct, identity-aligned contradiction.
- Before attributing any fact from a passage to the entity or relation named in `context`, confirm identity alignment (clear aliasing or explicit linking). Do not assume two similarly named mentions are the same without such alignment.
- If the `passages` consistently corroborate the context claim and are identity-aligned, append one brief paraphrased supporting sentence that cites the corroboration (keep the summary compact).
- If the `passages` are weak, ambiguous, or conflict with each other or with the context claim (but do not provide an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction), preserve the context claim and append a single short clause that flags the uncertainty or contradiction. When flagging, you may optionally include a verbatim quoted phrase from a passage of up to 10 words to show the source wording. Do not invent resolutions beyond what the `passages` show.
- If the `passages` contain an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction to the context claim, state that the passages contradict the context claim and briefly note the conflicting passage fact (optionally with a ≤10-word quote). Only in this case may the summary stop asserting the context claim as definitive.
- Keep the `summary` compact (aim for 1–3 sentences). Use uncertainty language only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not default to uncertainty when composition across context and passages licenses a conclusion.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from `context` and `passages` to form a licensed multi-hop conclusion; the supporting facts need not all appear in a single passage.
- Do not include long verbatim quotes, do not invent facts, and do not attempt to resolve contradictions beyond reporting them as present in the `passages`.

Output: a single `summary` string that follows the rules above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Use only the information in the provided summaries as evidence. summary_2 is the later, integrated evidence state and should be the primary source for deciding the minimal answer; summary_1 may contain useful first-hop compression or retrieval-oriented language and may be used to supply premises that, together with summary_2, license a conclusion. Do not invent facts or use outside knowledge.

Emit only the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries in the `answer` field. Do not include explanations, justification, or any additional text.

Preserve multi-hop composition: you may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts across the summaries to form a licensed conclusion even when no single sentence states the whole relation. Distinguish licensed composition from unsupported inference; emit uncertainty only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting in the summaries.

Minimal-answer formatting rule (explicit):
- If the summaries jointly support one minimal literal and that literal appears verbatim in the summaries and consists of multiple contiguous tokens (for example: a full personal name, a full date, or a place name), emit that full literal exactly as it appears in the summaries (verbatim, unchanged). Do not truncate or coerce it to a single token.
- If the jointly supported minimal answer is itself a single-token label, emit exactly that single token (in lowercase) and nothing else.

When in doubt about whether the summaries license a single definitive literal, prefer to emit uncertainty only if the required supporting premise or identity link is actually missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not make uncertainty the default when composition or transitive reasoning is required and the necessary premises are present.

Ensure the `answer` field contains only the minimal answer string supported by the summaries (subject to the formatting rule above).

hop1_docs: ... but the clouds ... | ... but the clouds ... is a television play by Samuel Beckett.  Beckett wrote it between October–November 1976 “to replace a film of "Play" which the BBC had sent [him] for approval (and which he had rejected)” due to “the poor quality of the film”.  Donald McWhinnie directed Billie Whitelaw and Ronald Pickup.  It was first broadcast on 17 April 1977 as part of a programme of three Beckett plays entitled ‘Shades’ on BBC2.  It was first published in "Ends and Odds" (Faber) 1977.  An early title for the piece was Poetry only love.

Ghost Trio (play) | Ghost Trio is a television play, written in English by Samuel Beckett.  It was written in 1975, taped in October 1976 and the first broadcast was on BBC2 on 17 April 1977 as part of the "Lively Arts" programme Beckett himself entitled "Shades".  Donald McWhinnie directed (supervised by Beckett) with Ronald Pickup and Billie Whitelaw.  The play’s original title was to be Tryst.  "On Beckett’s notebook, the word was crossed out vigorously and the new title "Ghost Trio" written next to it.  On the title page of the BBC script the same handwritten title change can be found, indicating that it must have been corrected at the very last minute."
Leo the Last | Leo the Last is a 1970 British drama film directed by John Boorman, based on the play "The Prince" by George Tabori, starring Marcello Mastroianni and Billie Whitelaw.

Private Schulz | Private Schulz is a BBC television comedy drama serial set mostly in Germany, during and immediately after World War II.  It stars Michael Elphick in the title role and Ian Richardson playing various parts.  Other notable actors included Tony Caunter, Billie Whitelaw, Billy Murray and Mark Wingett.
Ronald Pickup | Ronald Alfred Pickup (born 7 June 1940) is an English actor who has been active in television and film since 1964.

Shadey | Shadey is a 1985 British comedy film directed by Philip Saville and starring Antony Sher, Billie Whitelaw and Patrick Macnee.  The screenplay concerns a man with clairvoyant qualities who is recruited by British intelligence for a secret mission.  The film grossed over $65,817.
Tangiers (1982 film) | Tangier is a 1982 American-Moroccan thriller film directed by Michael E. Briant and starring Ronny Cox, Billie Whitelaw and Glynis Barber.
response.summary_1: Passages state that "... but the clouds ..." "is a television play by Samuel Beckett" and that "Donald McWhinnie directed Billie Whitelaw and Ronald Pickup"; it was "first broadcast on April 17, 1977" as part of the BBC programme "Shades" and was first published in "Ends and Odds" (Faber) 1977. The author (Samuel Beckett) is explicitly named in the evidence, so no additional retrieval is needed to identify the playwright.
response.hop2_query: "... but the clouds ..." Samuel Beckett Billie Whitelaw Ronald Pickup Donald McWhinnie
hop2_docs: ... but the clouds ... | ... but the clouds ... is a television play by Samuel Beckett.  Beckett wrote it between October–November 1976 “to replace a film of "Play" which the BBC had sent [him] for approval (and which he had rejected)” due to “the poor quality of the film”.  Donald McWhinnie directed Billie Whitelaw and Ronald Pickup.  It was first broadcast on 17 April 1977 as part of a programme of three Beckett plays entitled ‘Shades’ on BBC2.  It was first published in "Ends and Odds" (Faber) 1977.  An early title for the piece was Poetry only love.

Ghost Trio (play) | Ghost Trio is a television play, written in English by Samuel Beckett.  It was written in 1975, taped in October 1976 and the first broadcast was on BBC2 on 17 April 1977 as part of the "Lively Arts" programme Beckett himself entitled "Shades".  Donald McWhinnie directed (supervised by Beckett) with Ronald Pickup and Billie Whitelaw.  The play’s original title was to be Tryst.  "On Beckett’s notebook, the word was crossed out vigorously and the new title "Ghost Trio" written next to it.  On the title page of the BBC script the same handwritten title change can be found, indicating that it must have been corrected at the very last minute."

Footfalls | Footfalls is a play by Samuel Beckett.  It was written in English, between 2 March and December 1975 and was first performed at the Royal Court Theatre as part of the Samuel Beckett Festival, on May 20, 1976 directed by Beckett himself.  Billie Whitelaw, for whom the piece had been written, played May whilst Rose Hill voiced the mother.

Billie Whitelaw | Billie Honor Whitelaw, CBE (6 June 1932 – 21 December 2014) was an English actress.  She worked in close collaboration with Irish playwright Samuel Beckett for 25 years and was regarded as one of the foremost interpreters of his works.  She was also known for her portrayal of Mrs. Baylock, the demonic nanny in the 1976 horror film "The Omen".

From an Abandoned Work | From An Abandoned Work, a "meditation for radio" by Samuel Beckett, was first broadcast on BBC Radio 3’s Third Programme on Saturday, 14 December 1957 together with a selection from the novel "Molloy".  Donald McWhinnie, who already had a great success with "All That Fall", directed the Irish actor Patrick Magee.

Rough for Radio II | Rough for Radio II is a radio play by Samuel Beckett.  It was written in French in 1961 as Pochade radiophonique and published in "Minuit" 16, November 1975.  Beckett translated the work into English shortly before its broadcast on BBC Radio 3 on 13 April 1976.  Martin Esslin directed Harold Pinter (Animator), Billie Whitelaw (Stenographer) and Patrick Magee (Fox).  The English-language version was first published in "Ends and Odds" (Grove, 1976, Faber, 1977) as Radio II.

Embers | Embers is a radio play by Samuel Beckett.  It was written in English in 1957.  First broadcast on the BBC Third Programme on 24 June 1959, the play won the RAI prize at the Prix Italia awards later that year.  Donald McWhinnie directed Jack MacGowran – for whom the play was specially written – as "Henry", Kathleen Michael as "Ada" and Patrick Magee as "Riding Master" and "Music Master".  Robert Pinget translated the work as Cendres and "The first stage production was by the French Graduate Circle of Edinburgh, Edinburgh Festival, 1977."
response.summary_2: "... but the clouds ..." was written by Samuel Beckett; passage 1 corroborates this and also notes Donald McWhinnie directed Billie Whitelaw and Ronald Pickup for the BBC2 broadcast on 17 April 1977.
